userStories.title: Install @agentclientprotocol/sdk and create ACP client module
userStories.description: Add the ACP TypeScript SDK as a dependency and create a new src/core/acp-client.ts module that wraps the ClientSideConnection class. This module handles the ACP lifecycle: launch agent subprocess, initialize protocol handshake, create session, send prompts, and receive structured updates. Reference: https://agentclientprotocol.com/libraries/typescript
acceptanceCriteria: npm install @agentclientprotocol/sdk succeeds
New src/core/acp-client.ts with ACPClient class
ACPClient.launch() starts an agent subprocess and performs ACP handshake (Initialize + NewSession)
ACPClient.sendPrompt(sessionId, prompt) sends a prompt and returns structured SessionUpdate events
ACPClient.close() gracefully shuts down the session and process
Module is exportable but NOT wired into the main loop yet (standalone module)
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: ACP SDK provides ClientSideConnection for the client side (agent-cli) and AgentSideConnection for agents. We need ClientSideConnection. The SDK handles JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio transport. Reference: Compozy uses github.com/coder/acp-go-sdk v0.6.3 in Go — we use the TypeScript equivalent.

userStories.title: Implement ACP session lifecycle with structured update handling
userStories.description: Build the full session lifecycle on top of the ACP client: create session with working directory, send prompt as ContentBlock, subscribe to SessionUpdate notifications, parse structured content (text blocks, tool calls with start/update/completed/failed states, diffs, terminal output), detect session completion and idle states, handle errors with structured context.
acceptanceCriteria: Session creation accepts cwd and optional MCP servers config
Prompts sent as acp.TextBlock content blocks
SessionUpdate handler parses all content types: text, tool_use (start/update/completed/failed), diff, terminal_output
Tool call events include tool_call_id, tool name, and structured input/output
Diff events include old_text, new_text, and file path
Idle detection: session state transitions (working -> idle -> waiting_for_tool) are tracked
Session completion is detected from protocol (not string scanning)
Error handling uses structured ACP error codes, not process exit codes
All events emitted to the event bus (US-046 dependency) for journaling and UI
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: This replaces the current stream-json parsing in tool-runner.ts. ACP provides native structured updates instead of us parsing Claude-specific stream-json format. Compozy reference: internal/core/agent/client.go lines 400-600 (sessionUpdate handler with content block parsing).

userStories.title: Multi-agent support via ACP provider registry
userStories.description: Replace the current hardcoded tool registry (claude, openclaude) with an ACP provider registry. Each provider specifies: agent name, ACP adapter command (e.g. npx @agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp for Claude, npx @zed-industries/codex-acp for Codex), supported capabilities, and default MCP servers. Users can add new agents without code changes via config.
acceptanceCriteria: New src/core/acp-registry.ts with ACPProviderRegistry
Built-in providers: Claude (@agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp), Codex (@zed-industries/codex-acp), Copilot (@github/copilot --acp), Gemini (@google/gemini-cli --acp)
Each provider has: name, command, args, capabilities (fs, terminal), default env vars
Configurable via .agent-cli/providers.json or similar — users can add custom providers
CLI --tool flag maps to provider name (e.g. --tool codex)
Provider availability check (is the adapter installed?)
Fallback: if ACP adapter not found, suggest install command
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Reference: Compozy internal/core/agent/registry_specs.go has agent specs with Commands, Fallbacks, and EnvVars. ACP adapters are npm packages like @agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp. This makes agent-cli agent-agnostic.

userStories.title: MCP server integration per ACP session
userStories.description: ACP natively supports attaching MCP servers to sessions. Implement MCP server configuration per provider/project that gets passed during session creation. This allows agents to use additional tools (databases, APIs, filesystem extensions) beyond their built-in capabilities.
acceptanceCriteria: Session creation accepts mcpServers config matching ACP's MCP server format
MCP servers configurable per project (in prd.json or separate config)
MCP servers passed to NewSession request via the ACP SDK
Agent can use MCP tools during execution — tool calls appear in structured updates
Support at least: filesystem MCP server, custom tool MCP servers
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Reference: Compozy passes MCP servers in NewSessionRequest. This is a major advantage over the current approach — agents get extensible tools without modifying agent-cli. MCP server format: { command: string, args: string[], env?: Record<string, string> }.

userStories.title: Wire ACP client into the main iterator loop
userStories.description: Replace the current tool-runner.ts spawn+stdout approach with the ACP client for structured communication. The iterator creates an ACP session per iteration, sends the prompt as a structured ContentBlock, receives SessionUpdates, and detects completion from the protocol (no more <promise>COMPLETE</promise> string scanning). Keep tool-runner.ts as a legacy fallback for non-ACP tools.
acceptanceCriteria: Iterator detects if a tool has ACP support (via registry) and uses ACP client instead of tool-runner
ACP path: create session -> send prompt -> stream updates -> detect completion from protocol
Legacy path: existing tool-runner spawn+stdout (unchanged, for tools without ACP)
Story completion detection: instead of scanning stdout for <promise>COMPLETE</promise>, detect from ACP session result or agent's tool call that updates prd.json
Cost and duration extracted from ACP session metadata
Human-readable log still written to .agent-output.log
Monitor TUI shows ACP session updates (tool calls, diffs) in real time
Backward compatible: existing prd.json and workflows continue to work
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: This is the main integration story. The iterator should prefer ACP when available, fall back to legacy spawn+stdout when not. Compozy reference: internal/core/run/executor/runner.go uses ACP client per job execution. The <promise>COMPLETE</promise> signal can be replaced by checking if the agent's final tool call updated prd.json passes field.

userStories.title: ACP session resumption for interrupted runs
userStories.description: ACP supports loading existing sessions (LoadSession). Use this to implement proper session resumption instead of the current --resume which just tracks completed story IDs. When an iteration is interrupted, save the ACP session ID. On resume, load the session and continue from where the agent left off — no lost context, no repeated work.
acceptanceCriteria: On interruption (SIGINT, crash), save ACP session ID to .session.json
--resume flag loads the ACP session via LoadSession instead of starting a new one
Agent continues with full context from previous run (no prompt replay)
Session timeout handling: if session is expired, start a new one with a summary of what happened
Monitor detects resumed sessions and shows continuation in the UI
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Major improvement over current resume which just skips completed stories. ACP session resumption preserves the full agent context (conversation, tool state, working memory). Compozy reference: internal/core/agent/client.go LoadSession support.

userStories.title: Structured file system boundaries via ACP
userStories.description: ACP provides built-in file system capabilities with path validation. Use ACP's ReadTextFile/WriteTextFile operations instead of granting agents unrestricted filesystem access. Configure allowedRoots per session (project directory + optional add-dirs). All file operations are validated against allowed roots, preventing agents from accessing files outside their sandbox.
acceptanceCriteria: ACP session initialized with Fs capability (ReadTextFile, WriteTextFile)
allowedRoots set to project directory (+ projectDirectory if different)
Agent file operations are validated by ACP protocol — blocked if outside allowed roots
Path resolution: all paths resolved relative to session working directory
Replaces Docker sandbox for filesystem isolation (ACP does it at protocol level)
Optional: addDirs config for multi-directory projects
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Reference: Compozy internal/core/agent/client.go lines 863-958 (sandbox path resolution and validation). This is a security feature — agents can only touch files in the project directory without needing Docker.

userStories.title: Global agent-cli.md with per-project override support
userStories.description: Move the default agent-cli.md prompt template to ~/.agent-cli/agent-cli.md as a global default, eliminating the need to copy and maintain the file in every project directory. Projects can still override by having their own agent-cli.md in the working directory. The iterator and init module should check project-level first, then fall back to the global template.
acceptanceCriteria: Default agent-cli.md stored at ~/.agent-cli/agent-cli.md
~/.agent-cli/agent-cli.md is auto-created on first run if missing (from bundled template)
Iterator checks for project-level agent-cli.md first, falls back to global
--init copies from global template to project directory
Existing projects with agent-cli.md are unaffected (project-level takes precedence)
Single source of truth: update ~/.agent-cli/agent-cli.md to affect all projects without overrides
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: This solves the problem of having multiple copies of agent-cli.md that need to be updated independently. The global template is the default; per-project overrides are opt-in. Similar pattern to how providers.json works at ~/.agent-cli/providers.json.

userStories.title: Add stopWhen to PRD for declarative loop termination conditions
userStories.description: Add a `stopWhen` field to prd.json that lets users define custom conditions for when the agent loop should stop, beyond the existing all-stories-complete and max-iterations defaults. Support: stories (stop when specific story IDs complete), maxCostUsd (stop when accumulated cost exceeds threshold, ACP only), maxDurationMinutes (stop when session time exceeds limit). Conditions use OR logic — any condition being met triggers a stop. Also add a file deletion guard that detects and logs when the agent tries to delete files outside the project directory.
acceptanceCriteria: StopWhenCondition interface added to types.ts with stories, maxCostUsd, maxDurationMinutes fields
PRD interface extended with optional stopWhen field
prd.schema.json updated with stopWhen property definition
PRDManager.shouldStop() evaluates conditions with OR logic
Iterator checks stopWhen after each iteration (after stories check, before maxStories check)
File deletion guard: detect tool calls that delete files outside allowed roots, log warning to .agent-output.log and progress.log
isPathSafe() helper in file-utils.ts for path boundary checks
TEMPLATE_PRD in init.ts includes stopWhen example (empty/commented)
Backward compatible: missing stopWhen keeps current behavior
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Inspired by Vercel AI SDK's stopWhen callback. Since prd.json is static JSON, conditions are declarative (not callbacks). File deletion guard is safety-first: log but don't block initially, can add blocking later. Example stopWhen: { "stories": ["US-001"], "maxCostUsd": 5.0, "maxDurationMinutes": 30 }

userStories.title: Research and adopt AI SDK loop control patterns
userStories.description: Review the Vercel AI SDK's loop control primitives (maxSteps, stopWhen, onStepFinish, tool execution patterns) and evaluate which concepts can improve agent-cli's iteration loop. Focus on: conditional termination, step-level hooks, error recovery strategies, and structured progress reporting.
acceptanceCriteria: Document which AI SDK patterns apply to agent-cli's architecture
Identify gaps where agent-cli can improve beyond current patterns
Evaluate onStepFinish for potential per-iteration hooks (e.g., onIterationFinish callback)
Compare error recovery: AI SDK retry patterns vs agent-cli's current approach
List actionable improvements as future stories
userStories.notes: AI SDK reference: https://ai-sdk.dev/docs/agents/loop-control — maxSteps (equiv to maxIterations), stopWhen callback (equiv to our declarative stopWhen from US-068), onStepFinish hook. Look at how AI SDK handles tool results, multi-step reasoning, and structured output between steps.

userStories.title: Design and implement a session state reducer
userStories.description: Create a reducer pattern to manage session state across iterations. Currently state is scattered: session file (.session.json), PRD manager, iterator fields (iterationCount, storiesCompletedThisRun, acpTotalCost, acpTotalDuration, sessionCompletedIds). A reducer centralizes state transitions (story completed, iteration finished, session interrupted, branch changed, stopWhen triggered) into a single predictable state machine with an immutable state object. This makes state persistence, resumption, and observability straightforward — dispatch an action, get next state, persist it.
acceptanceCriteria: Define SessionState interface with all current state fields (iteration, completed stories, cost, duration, branch, ACP session ID, stopWhen status)
Define action types: STORY_COMPLETED, ITERATION_FINISHED, SESSION_INTERRUPTED, BRANCH_CHANGED, STOPWHEN_TRIGGERED, SESSION_RESUMED
Reducer function: (state, action) => nextState — pure, no side effects
Replace scattered state mutations in iterator.ts with dispatch calls
State persisted to .session.json after every dispatch (single write point)
Monitor/observability modules read from reducer state instead of aggregating from multiple sources
Backward compatible: existing .session.json files load into initial state
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Inspired by Redux-like reducer pattern and AI SDK's internal state management. Current pain points: (1) session state updated in 4+ places in iterator.ts, (2) cost/duration tracked as instance fields separate from session file, (3) resumption reconstructs state from multiple sources. A reducer solves all three. Future: could enable undo/redo, time-travel debugging, and state snapshots for the monitor TUI.

userStories.title: Add --story flag to target a specific story by ID
userStories.description: Add a --story <id> CLI flag that lets users run agent-cli for a single specific story instead of always picking the next highest-priority story. When set, the iterator skips priority ordering and works only on the given story ID. Validates that the ID exists in prd.json and that dependencies are met. Works with both --dry-run and live modes.
acceptanceCriteria: CLI flag --story <id> added to main command (e.g., agent-cli --story US-068)
AgentConfig type extended with optional storyId field
Iterator validates story ID exists in prd.json — error if not found
Iterator validates dependencies are met for the target story — error if blocked
When --story is set, iterator runs only that story (ignores priority ordering)
Works with --stories 1 to run exactly one iteration for the targeted story
status command shows which story would be targeted
Backward compatible: omitting --story keeps current priority-based behavior
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Currently getNextStory() always returns highest priority incomplete story with deps met. With --story, we bypass that and return the specific story. Supports multiple IDs comma-separated (e.g., --story US-068,US-070) to run specific stories in order. Useful for re-running a failed story, jumping ahead, or targeted testing.
